chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: FCC metallic structure with Au atoms at cube corners and face centers.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Naturally forms alloys with silver (electrum) and copper. High purity specimens have SG near 19.3; silver-rich electrum may be below 16.

crystallography.twinning: Spinel-law twins occasionally reported; generally rare.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Crystals rare; usually forms dendritic, arborescent, or wire habits. Well-formed octahedra occasionally found in hydrothermal veins.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Extremely malleable and ductile — can be beaten into leaf thinner than 100 nm. Does not tarnish or corrode under normal conditions. Highest malleability of any metal.

color_profiles.name: Pure Gold
color_profiles.cause: inherent electronic structure of metallic gold

color_profiles.name: Electrum
color_profiles.cause: natural silver alloy diluting gold color

appearance.visual_notes: Unmistakable warm golden-yellow in fresh specimens. Arborescent and dendritic forms are among the most prized mineral specimens globally.

formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Found on every continent. Primary deposits in Precambrian greenstone belts and quartz veins; secondary (placer) deposits in alluvial gravels downcurrent of primary deposits.

historical_uses: currency and coinage (earliest coins ~600 BCE, Lydia)
investment and financial reserves (gold standard era)
cultural_associations: solar deity in many traditions (Ra, Apollo, Surya)
divine perfection and immortality in alchemy
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Gold has been considered sacred across virtually every ancient civilization. The alchemical pursuit of turning base metals into gold (chrysopoeia) dominated medieval science. Ancient Egyptians considered gold the flesh of the gods. In Hindu tradition, gold is associated with Lakshmi and prosperity. The California Gold Rush (1848–1855) and Klondike Gold Rush (1896–1899) shaped modern North America.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Gold carries the weight of ten thousand years of human desire. It is simultaneously primordial and modern, terrestrial and cosmic.

practical_notes.toxicity: non-toxic in metallic form; gold salts (used in medicine) can be toxic in excess; generally considered biocompatible
practical_notes.handling_notes: Soft and malleable — specimens should not be stored with harder minerals. Avoid dropping.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm water and mild soap. Does not tarnish. Store separately from harder minerals.
treatments_or_synthetics: gold-filled and gold-vermeil jewelry distinct from solid gold
synthetic gold nanoparticles in industrial and medical use
common_confusions: pyrite ('fool's gold' — harder, brassy, cubic crystals or striated faces)
chalcopyrite (brassy yellow, harder, often iridescent tarnish)
biotite (dark flakes sometimes mistaken in panning)
practical_notes.market_notes: Most valuable of the commonly occurring native metal minerals. Spot price globally traded. Specimen gold (crystallized, dendritic, or nugget form) commands significant premium over melt value.

prompt_fragments: dendritic wire gold on white quartz matrix
nugget worn smooth by placer water
